content.text: Explain why the PlayerInfo card in the PlayerProfile layout always is 400px wide rather than filling the available width

content.text:
The width is hard-capped by an inline style, not by content or Mantine defaults.

In [PlayerProfile.tsx:94](src/components/PlayerProfile/PlayerProfile.tsx#L94), `PlayerInfo` is wrapped in:

```tsx
<Box style={{ flex: '1', maxWidth: '400px' }}>
```

`flex: '1'` is shorthand for `flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0%'`, which *would* let the box grow to fill the parent `Group`'s available space. But `maxWidth: '400px'` overrides that growth once the box reaches 400px — it's an explicit ceiling, so on any viewport wide enough to offer more than 400px per flex item, the box stops growing there instead of continuing to fill the row.

The same cap is applied to the `PlayerCard` box right above it ([line 84](src/components/PlayerProfile/PlayerProfile.tsx#L84)), so both stay pinned at 400px while `PlayerResults` and `PlayerPositions` are fixed at `w="14rem"` (224px, no flex-grow at all) — none of the four cards in that `Group` actually expand to consume leftover horizontal space, which is why the layout looks capped rather than fluid.

If you want `PlayerInfo` to fill available width, the fix is to drop or raise `maxWidth: '400px'` on its wrapping `Box` (and decide whether `PlayerCard` should keep its own cap or grow independently).

OK. I really need to get used to CSS rules. I didn't explain it properly. I think what I want to achieve is to have sensible minimum dimensions for components in the components themselves, but have them grow if it makes sense by controlling the parent layout. Given that, I want the four cards to be side by side in a full wide viewport and then gracefully stack as the container gets narrower - but with the cards scaling so that the right edges line up with the edge of the container
Let me look at the actual card components to see how they currently size themselves before proposing a fix.

That's a well-known flexbox pattern, and it's mostly what you're set up for already — you just need to apply it consistently. Here's the mechanism:

**Flexbox wrap + `flex-grow` on every card, `min-width` owned by each card.** When a flex container wraps, each row's items with `flex-grow: 1` expand to consume exactly the leftover space in that row — so the last item's right edge always lands on the container's right edge, no matter how many items fit per row. And `min-width` always wins over `flex-shrink`, so an item never shrinks below what it declares as its own minimum — once a row can't fit another item's minimum, it wraps to the next line. That's the "grow if it makes sense, respect minimums, stack gracefully" behaviour in one CSS rule combination, no media queries needed.

Currently your four cards are inconsistent:
- [PlayerCard.tsx:23](src/components/PlayerCard/PlayerCard.tsx#L23) and [PlayerInfo.tsx:50](src/components/PlayerInfo/PlayerInfo.tsx#L50) already declare their own `miw` (280px / 18rem) — good, that's the component owning its minimum.
- [PlayerResults.tsx:27](src/components/PlayerResults/PlayerResults.tsx#L27) and [PlayerPositions.tsx:28](src/components/PlayerPositions/PlayerPositions.tsx#L28) use a **fixed** `w="14rem"` instead — that's a hard width, not a minimum, so they can never grow.
- In the parent, only the PlayerCard/PlayerInfo wrapper `Box`es get `flex: 1` + a `maxWidth: 400px` cap ([PlayerProfile.tsx:84,94](src/components/PlayerProfile/PlayerProfile.tsx#L84)); PlayerResults/PlayerPositions get no flex treatment at all, and the 400px cap is exactly what stops growth once space is available.

The fix: change `w="14rem"` → `miw="14rem"` on Results/Positions (component-owned minimum, matching the other two), then in the parent wrap **all four** cards uniformly in `flex: '1 1 auto'` (no `maxWidth`) instead of just two of them with a cap. That's a purely parent-side change — the components never mention "grow," they only declare their own minimum.

Want me to make those changes across the four card components and PlayerProfile.tsx?

Right, but now what happens is each of the 4 cards is on its own row and has 100% width. What I want is for each card to be on the same row unless there's no room for its minimum width, BUT for the cards which DO fit on the row to add up to 100% width

Found it. `flex: '1 1 auto'` uses each card's **content-preferred width** as its starting size (the `auto` basis), not its declared minimum — so a card that visually wants more space than its `miw` claims a large hypothetical size, only one fits per row, and then that lone card's `flex-grow: 1` stretches to fill the entire row by itself. Repeat for each card and you get one-per-row at 100%.

The standard fix is to zero the flex-basis so growth starts from nothing and is shared equally, while `min-width` (already declared per-component) becomes the floor that both limits shrinking *and* drives the wrap decision:

content.text: I found the actual problem. Let me check the JSX nesting order — the flex style is on the wrong element.

Confirmed the bug: `DebugSizeOverlay` renders its own wrapper `<Box>` that becomes the actual flex item inside `Group`, and it has no `flex` style — so it doesn't grow. My `flex: '1 1 0'` `Box` is nested a level too deep (inside a non-flex parent), so it has no effect on the layout at all. I need to swap the nesting so the flex-styled `Box` is the direct child of `Group`, with `DebugSizeOverlay` wrapping inside it.

content.text:
# Run the project's mandatory finalisation checklist before reporting any task as done

## Steps

1. Run `npm run typecheck` and capture all output.
2. Run `npm run lint` and capture all output.
3. If either produced any errors or warnings:
   - For lint issues — run `npm run lint:fix` first to auto-fix what it can, then re-run `npm run lint` to see what remains.
   - Fix **every** remaining issue — do not skip, suppress, or dismiss any output.
   - If you are unsure whether a problem existed before your changes, run `git stash`, repeat both commands to establish a pre-change baseline, then `git stash pop` and fix only the new issues.
   - After fixing, re-run both commands to confirm they are clean.
4. Repeat until both commands exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings.

## Done criterion

`npm run typecheck` and `npm run lint` both exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings. Only then report the task as complete.

## Notes

- Fix issues in the order they appear — later errors are often caused by earlier ones.
- Avoid silencing a lint rule or adding a `@ts-ignore` / `eslint-disable` comment to make the output go away — fix the underlying problem. If a suppression is truly necessary (false positive, required vendor API), scope it as narrowly as possible (single line, specific rule) and add a short inline comment explaining why.
- If a typecheck error is in a generated file (`prisma/generated/`, `prisma/zod/`), do not edit it — regenerate it with `npx prisma generate` instead.

content.text:
Everything's clean and verified. Summary of the fix in [PlayerProfile.tsx](src/components/PlayerProfile/PlayerProfile.tsx):

- Changed `flex: '1 1 auto'` → `flex: '1 1 0'` on each card's wrapper `Box` — a zero basis means growth is shared equally rather than driven by each card's content-preferred width (which was causing every card to hog its own row).
- Swapped the nesting so the flex-styled `Box` is the direct child of the `Group`, with `DebugSizeOverlay` moved *inside* it. `DebugSizeOverlay` renders its own wrapper `<Box>` with no `flex` style — it was sitting between `Group` and my flex `Box`, so my flex rule never actually reached a real flex item and had no effect. That was the real reason nothing was growing.

Verified with Playwright against the Storybook story at several viewport widths: cards sit side by side and fill the row exactly at wide widths, drop to fewer-per-row as space tightens (each row's cards still growing to fill it), and stack one-per-row at narrow widths — right edges always flush with the container edge. `typecheck`, `lint`, and the `PlayerProfile` component tests all pass.

Now I've added a Group containing both the PlayerResults and PlayerPositions - I would like that to be wide enough for both on the same row if all the minimum widths allow, then to wrap into 2 rows if the container is too narrow.
Let me verify this actually works as intended by testing it live, since nested flex-wrap containers can behave subtly. I'll spin Storybook back up.

input.command:
npm run storybook > /tmp/storybook.log 2>&1 &
disown
for i in $(seq 1 30); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:6006 && break; sleep 1; done
input.description: Restart Storybook dev server
Measure Results/Positions card positions at various viewport widths

content.text:
The nested `Group`'s two children stack even at 1400px with plenty of room to spare. The cause: the outer `Group` splits space equally among its three flex items (`flex: '1 1 0'` each) purely by grow-ratio — it has no way to know the nested group *wants* more room to avoid an internal wrap, so it gives it exactly a third of the row (445px), which is less than the ~464px needed for both `PlayerResults` and `PlayerPositions` (14rem + 14rem + gap) side by side. The nested group's own automatic minimum, as far as the outer flex algorithm is concerned, is just its widest single child (224px) — because it's allowed to wrap internally — so the outer algorithm sees no violation and never grants it the extra space.

The fix is to give the nested `Group` an explicit `miw` covering both children's combined minimum, so the outer flexbox treats "wide enough for both side by side" as a hard floor rather than something it can shrink below before the outer row itself reflows.

                    </Box>
                    <Box style={{ flex: '1 1 0' }}>
                        <PlayerPositions player={player} year={year} record={record} />
                    </Box>
                </Group>
input.new_string:
{/* miw mirrors PlayerResults'/PlayerPositions' own 14rem miw: without it, the
                    outer flex-basis-0 split starves this group below the width both children
                    need side by side, so it wraps internally long before it needs to. */}
                <Group gap="md" wrap="wrap" style={{ flex: '1 1 0' }} miw="calc(28rem + var(--mantine-spacing-md))">
                    <Box style={{ flex: '1 1 0' }}>
                        <PlayerResults player={player} year={year} record={record} />
                    </Box>
                    <Box style={{ flex: '1 1 0' }}>
                        <PlayerPositions player={player} year={year} record={record} />
                    </Box>
                </Group>

Fresh-page layout measurement across widths with document width sanity check

result.output: The user doesn't want to proceed with this tool use. The tool use was rejected (eg. if it was a file edit, the new_string was NOT written to the file). STOP what you are doing and wait for the user to tell you how to proceed.

content.text: [Request interrupted by user for tool use]
No response requested.

The prev/next placeholder boxes no longer reserve space (previously navSlotWidth), so the title can shift horizontally depending on whether prevPlayer/nextPlayer exist. Restore a fixed width (or flex-basis) for the nav slots/placeholders to keep the header layout stable.
Don't use a fixed static width like before.

The stacking `Group + justify="space-between"` layout can't stay stable, because "space-between" just pushes each side item out by its own content width — an arrow link and an empty placeholder have different footprints, so the title (the middle item) drifts. A hardcoded width would fix that but you don't want a magic number.

The right tool here is CSS Grid with two identical `1fr` tracks flanking an `auto` title track: equal-`fr` tracks are *always* exactly equal to each other regardless of what's inside them, so the two nav slots stay symmetric and the title stays centered — no magic number, self-balancing by construction.

input.old_string:
<Group justify="space-between" gap="xs" mb="lg">
                <Box ta="center">
                    {prevPlayer ?
                        <PlayerLink player={prevPlayer} year={year} format="left-arrow" /> :
                        <Box data-testid="player-prev-placeholder" aria-hidden="true" />}
                </Box>
                <TitleWithYearDropdown order={1} title={player.name} year={year} validYears={activeYears} />
                <Box ta="center">
                    {nextPlayer ?
                        <PlayerLink player={nextPlayer} year={year} format="right-arrow" /> :
                        <Box data-testid="player-next-placeholder" aria-hidden="true" />}
                </Box>
            </Group>
input.new_string:
{/* Equal 1fr side tracks always stay the same width as each other, so the
                title stays centred whether or not a nav link is present - no fixed
                width needed. */}
            <Box
                mb="lg"
                style={{
                    display: 'grid',
                    gridTemplateColumns: 'minmax(0, 1fr) auto minmax(0, 1fr)',
                    alignItems: 'center',
                    gap: 'var(--mantine-spacing-xs)',
                }}
            >
                <Box ta="center">
                    {prevPlayer ?
                        <PlayerLink player={prevPlayer} year={year} format="left-arrow" /> :
                        <Box data-testid="player-prev-placeholder" aria-hidden="true" />}
                </Box>
                <TitleWithYearDropdown order={1} title={player.name} year={year} validYears={activeYears} />
                <Box ta="center">
                    {nextPlayer ?
                        <PlayerLink player={nextPlayer} year={year} format="right-arrow" /> :
                        <Box data-testid="player-next-placeholder" aria-hidden="true" />}
                </Box>
            </Box>

content.text: `typecheck` and `lint` both pass. Summary: replaced the header `Group justify="space-between"` with a `Box` using CSS Grid (`minmax(0, 1fr) auto minmax(0, 1fr)`), so the two nav slots are always exactly equal width to each other by construction — no fixed pixel/rem value, self-balancing regardless of whether a link or an empty placeholder occupies each side. Verified live that the slots stay pixel-equal even with deliberately asymmetric content injected into one side.

Typing positionStyles as Record<string, CSSProperties> loses type-safety for position and can hide missing keys. Prefer Record<NonNullable<DebugSizeOverlayProps['position']>, CSSProperties> (or a dedicated union type) so positionStyles[position] is guaranteed to exist at compile time.
